projects.title: Laser-Guided Lance Alignment Mechanism
projects.description:
Introduction: LD#1 converters face frequent downtime and hot metal loss due to improper center alignment of the oxygen lance during changeover and maintenance. Accurate center lance alignment is essential for stable blowing, to avoid tip burn/puncture and associated vessel incidents, and to reduce unplanned stoppages. The proposed concept introduces a simple, laser-based reference system that makes lance alignment fast, repeatable and largely operator-independent.
Conventional Process: Alignment is done by trial-and-error from the operating platform. Operators rely on visual judgement and repeated traversing of the lance carriage. Typical alignment cycle: 50–55 minutes per lance change (average ~52 min).
Proposed System: Laser-based center lance alignment involves installing vertical laser sheets near the lance handling area to create a fixed reference plane. A laser alignment target/plate is mounted on the lance, and a plumb (or equivalent) is used to validate verticality. Lance position is adjusted until its axis coincides with the laser plane at predefined reference marks. Magnetic/clamped fixtures are used for laser modules to allow quick mounting and removal. A standardized SOP converts the activity into a quantitative, reference-based process with minimal trials.
Challenges: RCFA FY25 shows ~2,509 minutes/year of lance-related downtime. Production loss equivalent to ~38.5 heats/year. Hot metal loss = 38.5 heats × 160 t/heat = ~6,160 t/year. Misalignment increases risk of tip burn/puncture. Adds mechanical stress on rope, gearbox, and carriage systems.
Benefits & Business Potential: Alignment time reduced from 52 min to 22 min (avg). Lance-related downtime expected to reduce by 40-50%. Recovery of 15-20 heats/year. Hot metal throughput recovery ~2,775-3,700 t/year. Low-cost retrofit using standard laser modules.
Uniqueness of the approach: Converts subjective, operator-based alignment into a clear, reference-driven process. Uses simple laser-sheet hardware with magnetic fixture. Portable setup usable across multiple vessels and lances in LD#1.
Impact: Boosts converter throughput by reducing lance-related downtime 40-50%.

projects.title: Predictive Detection of Segment Roll Standstill
projects.description:
Introduction: Continuous casting uses ~15 segments, 20 rolls each, ~300 non-driven rolls/caster. Non-driven rolls guide slabs but can jam due to wear or contamination. Jammed rolls risk marks, quality loss & downtime. Analysis shows roll jamming accounts for ~84% of slabs held. Roll jam effect: Stationary roll scratches slab → groove marks.
Conventional Process: Jamming detected only after groove marks appear. No roll-wise visibility → complete segment must be opened. Minimum 4-8 hours downtime → ₹2.8-5.7 crore loss.
Proposed Solution: Magnetic reed sensor + magnet on each roll. Real-time speed & slip detection. PLC integration → alerts for exact roll number. Predictive maintenance instead of reactive.
Block Diagram: The system uses a 'Roll + Magnet' which interacts with a 'Reed Sensor'. The sensor output is 'Pulse Sensing', which is then 'Pushed to the HMI in Real Time' and 'Integrated with PLC'. The PLC output is 'Aligned with Pre-Calibrated reference value'. The diagram also shows 'Driven Rolls', 'Pinch Rolls', and 'Non-Driven' rolls within 'Segment Frame' with 'Clamping Cylinders' and 'Casting Direction' indicated, showing 'Gap entrance' and 'Gap exit' for the 'Slab'.
Solution Architecture: A 'Segment Roll' has a 'Physical Mounting' for a 'Sensor' and 'Magnet'. The sensor connects to a 'Controller' via 'Wired Transmission' or 'Wireless Transmission'. The controller sends data via 'MQTT Protocol' to 'GCP' (Google Cloud Platform) which connects to 'ANI & Tableau' for visualization.
Benefits: 70-80% reduction in roll-related defects. Prevents groove marks, partial cooling, surface scratches. Major reduction in downtime cost (₹72 lakhs/hr). Targeted maintenance → open the segment selectively.
Uniqueness: First system providing roll-wise real-time visibility. Rugged sensors suited for high heat, water & EMI. Low-cost, passive, reliable solution.
Way Forward: Deploy across all caster segments. Integrate with PLC + central dashboard. Real Time Monitoring in HMI. Standardize & expand plantwide.
Impact: Cuts roll-related slab defects by 70-80%, recovering major casting uptime.

projects.title: Optimising Gas Filtration for CV Analyser
projects.description:
Introduction: Sludge, clogging, and corrosion cause unstable sampling and wrong CV readings. Diesel scrubber is hazardous, causes corrosion and requires frequent maintenance. Tar and naphthalene condensation raises pressure drop which leads to maintenance per shift.
Proposed System: Replace diesel with a wash oil scrubber. Introduce a 4-stage filtration by adding a sintered metal filter. In-house making of the whole filtration system to reduce dependence on the vendor. Cuts corrosion, manual cleaning, and extends filter life.
Benefits & Business Potential: Environment-friendly, human-safe filtration system leading to less carbon emissions. Less SRM & manpower, better ARC consolidation. Less corrosion leading to longer shelf life. Low-maintenance, fewer service calls. Wash oil absorbs coal tar completely, unlike diesel which saturates quickly. Wash oil is in-house produced; there is no capital involved necessarily. Reuse of wash oil minimises overall waste generation.
Challenges: In-house availability may fluctuate, but outsourced wash oil still remains cheaper than diesel.
Future Scope: 3 & 4-stage systems with sintered housing made internally to cut costs. Filter elements can be sourced from M/s Gopani Filters, Surat, for quality & reliability.
Recurring Maintenance Benefit Analysis: Optimized monthly no. of visits required per systems: 6. Optimized yearly maintenance cost of 25 systems: 22.5 Lakhs. Yearly maintenance cost saving of 1000 filters: 9 Crore.
Impact: Boosts filter life and cuts maintenance visits, saving 9 Cr per year.

projects.title: Increasing Coal Recovery from Highwall Paste Filling
projects.description:
Introduction: Highwall mining extracts only 30–37% of coal due to stability requirements. Paste backfilling can increase recovery to 80–85%, ensuring stability and sustainability.
Proposed System: Fly ash + OB mixed with cement and water to create paste. Pumped through vertical boreholes into highwall voids. Barricading at gallery mouth using OB rock to ensure paste confinement.
Block Diagram: The diagram illustrates the process. 'OVERBURDEN' is at the top, followed by 'PASTE INJECTION' through a 'VERTICAL BOREHOLE'. Below this is the 'COAL SEAM' and 'BLOCKED COAL (PILLAR)'. The paste fills the voids, resulting in 'PASTE FILLED & SOLIDIFIED' and 'WEB CUT (PASTE FILLING)'.
Paste Mixing Setup: This diagram shows the components for paste mixing: 'FLYASH', 'CEMENT', 'SANDSTONE', 'OVERBURDEN', and 'BINDERS' are stored in separate silos. These are fed into a mixing tank, from which the 'PASTE MIXING SETUP' produces the final paste.
Benefits: Enables higher percentage of coal extraction from highwall area. Stabilizes highwall and prevents subsidence. Utilizes fly ash and OB, reducing waste dumping. Increased profit margin in Highwall mining sector.
Challenges: Tracking the paste solidification time. How to control the flow of the paste along the dip. Barricading strength and leakage prevention. Limited to only 40m OB strata length using conventional drilling techniques.
Uniqueness: First of its kind application in highwall mining in India. Integrates waste utilization with mine recovery.
Way Forward: Execution of this technique in the bottom seams and to improve the operational efficiency.
Impact: Boosts coal recovery from 30-37% to 80-85% through highwall paste filling.

projects.title: Acoustic Monitoring of Cylinder Leakage
projects.description:
Introduction: Hydraulic cylinders are crucial for the precise operation of Hot Strip Mill. Internal leakage, a hidden fault, severely impacts performance and can lead to costly failures. Current detection methods are often reactive, identifying issues mainly after significant problems arise. This project introduces a proactive monitoring approach to ensure enhanced reliability and efficiency.
Conventional Process: It primarily relies on detection methods like visual inspection, thermography and use of Ultraprobe. These methods are mostly reactive, identifying issues only after significant performance degradation. This means problems are often caught late, leading to unplanned downtime and efficiency losses.
Proposed Solution: Acoustic sensors are installed on hydraulic cylinders to capture sound signatures of oil flow. These signals are analyzed to identify distinct acoustic wave patterns corresponding to different conditions. This enables early, predictive detection of internal leakage, shifting from reactive to predictive maintenance.
Block Diagram: The process starts with 'Install AE sensor on cylinder'. Signals then 'Pass signals through pre-amp' and are 'Store signals in DAQ'. From DAQ, 'Frequency and time domain analysis' is performed. This analysis is verified with 'Verifying analysis with actual situation', which feeds back to 'Fine tuning the prediction model'.
Challenges: Installation of sensors can be tricky as we are dealing with various kinds of cylinders. Scaling the project poses a significant financial hurdle, with approximately 990 hydraulic cylinders in HSM and 50,000 actuators across Tata Steel needing monitoring. Managing data from a vast sensor network across multiple locations like TSJ, TSM, and TSK presents logistical and data integration challenges.
Benefits: Early and accurate detection of internal leakage prevents unexpected failures and ensures precise control in HSM operations. It significantly improves the reliability and stability of the hot strip mill, reducing unplanned downtime and operational disruptions. It enables predictive, data-driven maintenance, leading to substantial cost savings by avoiding production losses and optimizing asset lifespan.
Uniqueness: It uniquely uses sound patterns from internal oil flow to diagnose seal health, offering a non-traditional and non-invasive approach. The system identifies the onset of leakage long before performance degradation becomes evident, unlike conventional visual or thermal methods. This transforms maintenance from reactive repairs to a data-driven, predictive strategy, preventing costly unexpected failures and providing objective insights into a hidden fault.
Way Forward: Installation and standardization of process at HSM. Installation at other shopfloors which heavily depend on hydraulic cylinders.
Impact: Early detection of internal leakages reduces unplanned downtime, prevents cylinder failures, and improves HSM reliability, resulting in significant maintenance savings, enhanced productivity, and a shift from reactive repairs to a proactive, cost-efficient monitoring system.

projects.title: HSM Furnace Door Reliability Improvement
projects.description:
Introduction: The door operates through a chain-tie rod system that undergoes high thermal and mechanical stress, causing tie-rod bending and fatigue. This leads to failures at threading between chain joint and tie rod, and if the chain falls onto the roller table, causing major downtime. A mechanism to prevent tie-rod fall and improve furnace door reliability is required.
Timeline of the breakdowns (Recorded data from September 2020 to June 2025): The table lists 5 major delays in hours (>30 min) from Sep-20 to Jun-25. For example, in Sep-20, FCE#3 charging door both side door hanging chain turn buckle assembly got broken, leading to 6:23 hours delay. In Jun-25, FCE#3 not in service, Tie Rod broken, leading to 11:00 hours delay.
Proposed System: A roller support frame arrangement mounted on an I-beam support directly above and below the chain path to arrest the chain movement—a fully mechanized solution. When a break occurs, the chain joint gets mechanically trapped within the roller gap, preventing chain fall and reducing downtime by eliminating the additional steps like scaffolding, U-sealing, the need for furnace to cool.
Benefits & Business Potential: MTTR can be reduced by 6 hours. Simplifies repair and maintenance process. Financial savings per breakdown: 5–7 hrs × 15 lakhs/hr i.e. ₹75 lakh to ₹1.05 crore saving per incident. Safety improvement: Zero scaffolding requirement, reduced furnace exposure, lower risk to manpower since we are preventing the issue of chain from falling on to the roller table. Operational reliability: Planned maintenance to reduce downtime, stable production planning.
Challenges: Pilot implementation. Integrating the roller arrangement and ensuring precise placement in order.
Future Scope: Incorporation of an auxiliary mechanism to hold gate in case of failure. Integration of sensor, capable of sustaining high temp, in the vicinity of the most susceptible regions on chains.
Impact: Reduces furnace-door failures and saves ₹75 lakh-₹1.05 Cr per incident by preventing chain-fall breakdowns, cutting MTTR by six hours, avoiding scaffolding costs, and improving planned maintenance reliability for stable HSM operations.

projects.title: Design & Development of Inspection Riveting Marking M/C
projects.description:
Introduction: The manufacturing process of bearings begins with the careful selection of raw materials and progresses through various stages of machining, heat treatment, assembly and final inspection. Bearing manufacturers are increasingly incorporating advanced technologies, robotic automation, and digital inspection systems to improve efficiency, precision, and quality control in the manufacturing process. At Tata Bearings, the inspection riveting marking (IRM) machine is a specialized, integrated system that performs three critical final assembly and quality functions in a single automated cell: Inspection (Post-Assembly Quality Check), Riveting (Cage Fastening), and Marking (Traceability & Branding).
Proposed System: In-house design, develop and implement a new IRM machine for the Medium and Large ball bearing (MLBB) in assembly section. Increasing MLBB bearing production to meet market demand effectively.
Benefits: Increased productivity (Setting time reduced) and efficiency (Aligned with the Grinding module). Optimized resource utilization.
Uniqueness: Cost savings through in-house design and manufacturing.
Way Forward: The operation is carried out manually, so exploring potential for further automation to eliminate the Man-machine interface. To develop IRM machine for small Ball Bearing for outer dia. up to 62 mm.
Impact: Improves efficiency and cuts costs via faster, in-house automated IRM.

projects.title: SmartShift FC9 Shuttle: Bin-Driven Automation
projects.description:
Problem Statement: There are four bins in Jig each of capacity 250 T. When Jig needs to be operated, Fines from wash plant is diverted towards jig and filled in bins with help of FC9 Conveyor which is a shuttle conveyor. Once one bin is filled the conveyor needs to be shifted. This operation is operator based. If operator is not vigilant, the bins overflow, jamming the conveyor and causing detention. In order to eliminate the operator-based operation of the conveyor, we will automate the process in such a way that the logic shall automatically shift the conveyor based on bin levels.
Current Condition: FC9 is a conveyor that fills the four Jig Bins. The conveyor supports both reversible and traverse motion. The bins are clubbed as Bin 1 & 3 and Bin 2 & 4. When conveyor is placed at any one of the club, the conveyor is reversed to fill the next bin of the club and then the conveyor is shifted to another bin club. The whole process is man (operator) dependent who operates it via SCADA. If the operator is not vigilant then bins fill and overflow, jamming the conveyor and its movement.
Counter Measures: Installation of chute level sensors. Logic development and implementation so that if for a pair the bin level goes beyond 90% conveyor reverses. If both the bins of a pair bin fill, the conveyor shifts to another pair. If all the bin level goes beyond 90%, the feeding circuit stops and alarm is triggered.
Jig Plant Flow Sheet: The diagram shows the flow of material from 'SLURRY TANK' to 'JIG FEED HOPPER' via 'CDWS' and 'UIF'. The 'FC-9 SHUTTLE CONVEYOR' fills four bins (BWF 1-4) which have level sensors (LS1-LS4). The 'JIG OPERATION' section includes 'VF-1', 'VF-2', 'FC-10', 'FC-12', 'FC-13', 'RDWS', and 'REJECTS'.
WHY-WHY Analysis: Problem: Jamming of jig bins due to material overflow thereby unable to shift the shuttle conveyor and stopping the plant. Philosophy: Based on the high bin level, the belt was not shifted/reversed which led to the problem. What's Wrong: This is a manual process where operator has to keep a look on the bin levels and based on it he takes decision. If he is unaware or engaged in other job, the problem occurs. Pain Point: No automation exists. Solution: The conveyor shall make auto decisions based on jig bin level and the conveyor will change its material filling points based on logic. In logic high bin level means the bin has material >=80% of net capacity. Bin 1 and Bin 3 form one pair and Bin 2 and Bin 4 forms another. If one bin of a pair is full, the belt reverses to fill next bin of the pair and if both bins of a pair is full, the belt travels a traverse distance to fill another pair. If all 4 bins are about to be full (bin level>80%), an alarm will pop up in SCADA alarming bin level high. Thereafter it may be taken in manual mode with a Manual and Auto button available in SCADA display.
Results: Automation of a process. Reduced dependency on operator. Reduced cost of cleaning. Elimination of chute and belt jam due to material.
Impact: Prevents chute jams and overflow by automating shuttle-conveyor shifting.

projects.title: Cold Blast Dehumidification for Blast Furnace
projects.description:
Introduction: When moisture in the hot blast air increases, it creates several problems: Decreases blast furnace stability and production. Increases coke rate consumption and decreases PCI. Increases blower power and stove power consumption. Increases corrosion of ducts and other parts.
Proposed Solution: The system uses a reverse Carnot refrigeration cycle to cool air to its dew point, condensing water vapor into liquid. A 40KV Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) ionizes and captures any residual micro water droplets splashed into the air. Arduino-controlled dampers ensure the synchronized opening and closing of the dehumidifying and bypass ducts to prevent pressure changes. Copper coils with blue aluminum TiO_2 coated fins and precooling tubes are used to reach an absolute humidity of 10g/Nm^3. The layout includes a bypass duct and monitoring probes to maintain blast furnace stability during unit maintenance or startup.
Benefits: Coke cost savings up to ₹42 crores and lowering the coke rate by 2%. An increase in production by 1%, resulting in an annual profit gain of up to ₹10.5 crores. Decreased energy consumption in blower house by 9.4% saving up to ₹10.7 crores. Producing 48 lakh gallons of distilled water worth ₹8.73 crores. Higher usage rate of PCI. Increased stability of Blast furnace. Total Value generated up to ₹68 crores/annum.
Challenges: In case of filter failure, risk of dust and condensate forming sludge, mitigated by optimized fin spacing and in-situ washing. Risk of added pressure drop affecting blower stability, mitigated by low-velocity design and auto-bypass dampers.
Way Forward: Perform CFD and thermal modelling to optimize airflow, heat transfer and overall efficiency. Explore advanced surface coatings to improve condensation and reduce fouling.
Impact: Reduces energy use and recovers 48 lakh gallons of water, boosting sustainable BF operation.

projects.title: Plastic to Fuel via Electric Pyrolysis
projects.description:
Problem Statement: The Industrial Byproduct Management Department (IBMD) currently incurs costs by lending money to vendors for plastic waste and scrap disposal, leading to an issue economically as well as environmentally. The current level of disposal of plastics by IBMD is 120 tons annually.
Conventional Method: Currently IBMD gives plastics to the vendor for the disposal and, also they lend money for disposal.
Proposed Unique Solution: Plastic is made up of multiple crosslinked hydrocarbon polymer chains. Pyrolyzing these polymers at certain conditions could potentially break the linkages between them and produce hot polymer vapors at high temperature. These polymers could be condensed to form fuel, which could then be used as energy source for various purposes.
Block Diagram: The process starts with 'Plastic feeding', which goes into 'Pyrolyzing'. The output is 'Cooling of gases in condenser'. From the condenser, 'Collection of oil' and 'Filtering of wax' are performed.
Potential Benefits: No expenses or costs incurred to dispose plastic and instead, the fuel produced could function as a revenue generating platform. Easily adapted to small-scale or large-scale applications according to the necessity. Relatively implementable and feasible, as it involves no integration with other machines. 2 hrs of operation over 300 operating days in 500 kg capacity chamber can earn a revenue of 1.56 Cr/annum.
Future Scope: Waste heat energy could be used to preheat the air and pass it through the heater of reactor for a better energy efficiency. Fuel could be distilled for further classification of the oils in it. Residual gases could be reused to heat the reactor whereas the black char could be laid along with the roads for pollution free disposal.
Impact: Transforms plastic waste into fuel, cutting disposal burden and generating revenue.

projects.title: Autonomous Solar Oil Skimmer
projects.description:
Introduction & Problem Statement: Bifacial solar panels are installed as floating units on the upper cooling pond. They generate power from direct sunlight on the top surface and reflected light on the bottom surface. Oil present in the pond adheres to the lower surface of the panels, blocking reflected light. This fouling reduces panel efficiency to about 12%, compared with the global average of around 25%.
Solution: 1. Installing Oil Booms: Oil booms are the floating barriers installed around solar panels to contain surface oil. They prevent oil from spreading and reaching the lower surface of panels. 2. Autonomous Oil Skimmer boat: Self operating boat designed to remove surface oil. Rotating belt made of oleophilic material moves through the water surface. Oil adheres to the belt and carried upward. Collected oil is scrapped into storage container for safe disposal.
Benefits & Business Potential: Energy Performance: Efficiency Gain: +5%. Monthly Output: +50 MWH. Revenue Generation: Monthly Revenue: Rs 1.60 Lakhs. Annual Benefit: Rs 19.26 Lakhs.
Impact: Improves solar efficiency by 5% using an automated, sustainable oil-skimming system.

projects.title: Reduction of SPM Emissions from Coke Oven Battery
projects.description:
Background: The NINL Coke Oven Battery, commissioned in 2004, underwent a shutdown in 2020 without prior mitigation measures. It was recommissioned in September 2023 following repairs to 4-6 flues. Currently, elevated SPM emissions are observed from the chimney due to aging and operational constraints. This project aims to analyze the SPM carryover mechanism and implement feasible mitigation measures without impacting production.
Problem Statement: Ageing battery with prolonged thermal deterioration. Leading to cross-leakages between carbonizing chambers and heating flues. Fine dust particles carried along with waste flue gas and travels through the flue tunnel to the chimney. Resulting in elevated SPM values (Average SPM ~55 mg/Nm3 and frequent SPM alerts (~27 per day) at the stack.
Methodology adopted: The methodology involves 'Cause Identification & Validation', 'Design Finalization & Procurement Action', 'Implementation of System', and 'Monitoring & Sustenance of the System'.
Representative schematic for understanding SPM carryover mechanism: The schematic shows 'Charging Car' and 'Ascension pipe' leading to 'CO Gas' and 'GC main'. 'Operating bench' and 'Anchorage' are shown. 'Dust-laden Flue Gas' from 'Waste heat Canal' and 'Waste heat box' goes to 'Flue Tunnel' and then to 'BPP' (Baghouse Pulse Jet Filter) and 'Tar Free CO Gas'. 'Elevated SPM Values & SPM Alerts' are indicated.
Implemented Solution: Detailed study of SPM generation and dust carryover mechanism. Evaluation of practical emission reduction options suitable for ageing battery. Selection of dust suppression approach without structural chimney modification. Design and installation of Water Spray Dust Suppression System inside chimney. The Project is implemented for the 1st time in India.
Challenges: Ageing battery with limited scope for major refractory repair during operation. Cross-leakages cannot be eliminated without extended shutdown.
Benefits & Business Potential: Significant reduction in SPM alerts (30-40% reduction in stack alerts). Improved environmental compliance.
Way Forward: Exploring zero emissions with additional Measures: Regular dusting and ceramic welding at leakage points. Flue grouting to minimize cross-leakages. Daily spray flushing & nozzle inspection.
Impact: Brings down recurring suspended particulate matter and improving compliance by 30-40%, improving air quality.

projects.title: Reliability Improvement of Sample Handling System
projects.description:
Introduction: The Sample Handling System (SHS) conditions raw process gas by controlling temperature, moisture, pressure, and flow before analysis. Reliable SHS operation is critical for automation, interlocking, and gas recovery across BF, LD, Coke Plant, and Mills. Any instability directly impacts analyzer availability and process continuity.
Conventional Process / Existing Gaps: Sample flow measured manually using rotameters. No real-time feedback, alarm, or interlock for low-flow conditions. Flow degradation detected only after analyzer trip. Reactive troubleshooting instead of preventive action. RCFA shows repeated flow-related and probe choking failures.
Problem Statement: Drop in sample flow below threshold leads to analyzer trips causing: LD gas recovery miss. BF PCI injection interruption. CDQ bunkering interruption. Blowing miss and blow heat miss.
FY-25 impact: 6 LD blowing misses, 4 BF PCI interruptions, 2 blow heat misses → ₹1.9 Cr loss due to unreliable SHS operation.
Proposed Solutions (RCFA-Based): Dual sample probe with manual changeover → Ensures uninterrupted sampling during probe choking or failure. Hot redundancy for SHS power & critical modules → Instantaneous switchover without analyzer shutdown. Primary Solution: Real-Time Gas Flow Monitoring.
Proposed Solution - System Concept: The block diagram shows 'Raw Process Gas' flowing to a 'Manual Flow Element'. This is monitored by a 'Rotational Speed Flow Sensor' which sends signals to 'Signal Conditioning & Display'. The 'Analyzer PLC' receives input from 'Signal Conditioning & Display' and outputs a '4-20 mA Output'. The system includes 'Digital Rotameter' and 'CDI 5100 Flow Meter'.
Technical Highlights: Series-mounted rotational speed-based flow sensing element. Signal linearized and converted to standard 4-20 mA. Plug-and-play integration with existing PLC. Enables alarms, trends, and future data logging. No major SHS modification required.
Challenges Addressed: Undetected flow degradation. Delayed choke identification. Analyzer trips due to blind operation. Repeated flow-related SHS failures.
Benefits: Real-time flow visibility and early warning. Reduction in analyzer trips and process interruptions. Preventive maintenance instead of reactive action. Improved SHS availability and process reliability. Digital flow meter functionality at ~25-30% cost.
Uniqueness / Novelty: Cost-optimized alternative to digital flow meters. Converts a manual, open-loop system into a monitored closed-loop system. Scalable across multiple SHS points with minimal CAPEX. Directly derived from RCFA, Pareto, and trend analysis.
Way Forward: Pilot implementation on critical SHS points. PLC-based alarm optimization and trip logic. Long-term trend analysis for predictive choking detection. Plant-wide replication across BF, LD, and Mills.
Financial Impact: Cost per unit of Solution: 61,600/-. Total Cost Incurred: 3.58 Cr/-. Savings = 12.1 Cr/-.
Impact: The system reduces SHS downtime costs and saves 3.58 Cr through proactive monitoring and early fault detection. By preventing analyzer trips and flow-related failures, it minimizes high-impact interruptions and avoids expensive shutdowns.

projects.title: Reduction Of Idle Freight in Rapid Loading System
projects.description:
Introduction: Despite accurate loading within a 1% tolerance band, the rake consistently departs with total chargeable weight (actual mass discharged by the RLS gates) below the government-permitted capacity, causing idle freight and cost inefficiency.
Proposed System: Implement an operator-in-loop dynamic loading adjustment system with a real-time dashboard that monitors cumulative weight and alerts the operator to redistribute residual tonnage across remaining wagons by adjusting gate open time.
Cost Considerations: Daily Snapshot (Nov 1, 2025) shows Permitted Cty. 4560 ton, Chargeable Cty. 4468.8 ton, Net Qty. 4441.3 ton, Difference: 27.5 ton, Idle Freight: 0.62%. Monthly Overview (Nov 2025) shows Chargeable Qty. 989050 ton, Net Qty. 977661.83 ton, Difference: 11388.17 ton, Rakes Loaded: 229, Extra Rakes: 2.49 (~3), Idle Freight: 1.16%.
Financial Impact Analysis: Monthly Cost (Nov '25): ₹30,40,641.39 (Approx.). Annual Projected Cost (at 1.16% avg.): ₹3,64,87,696.68 (Approx.). Cost: (Rs. 2.67/ton/km x 11388.17 ton x 100 km). Rough Distance: 100 km.
Block Diagram: The system flow starts with 'START: New Rake Arrival (N=58 Wagons)'. It then 'INITIALIZE PARAMETERS: Total Target = 4560T, Wagon Count = 58'. Next, 'CALCULATE INITIAL TARGET PER WAGON: 4560T/58 = 78.62T' and 'SET INITIAL PLC SETPOINT'. The 'BEGIN WAGON LOADING CYCLE (Wagon i=1 to 58)' involves 'LOAD WAGON "I" (RLS Operation)', 'MEASURE ACTUAL WEIGHT (W_actual)', 'UPDATE CUMULATIVE TOTAL LOAD: Total_Load = Total_Load + W_actual'. Then, 'CALCULATE REMAINING LOAD & WAGONS: 4560T-Total_Load; 58-i' and 'CALCULATE NEW SETPOINT: Remaining Load / Remaining Wagons'. A decision point 'IS NEW SETPOINT > MAX SAFETY LIMIT (e.g., 80T)?' leads to 'TRIGGER "CRITICAL UNDERLOAD ALARM" ON DASHBOARD' and 'CAP SETPOINT AT MAX LIMIT (Requires Manual Intervention)' if yes, or 'DISPLAY NEW TARGET ON OPERATOR DASHBOARD (Safe Adjustment)' and 'OPERATOR ADJUSTS PLC SETPOINT FOR NEXT WAGON' if no. The cycle continues with 'INCREMENT WAGON COUNT (i = i + 1)' and 'IS i > 58 (All Wagons Loaded)?'. If yes, it's 'END: RAKE LOADING COMPLETE (Final Weight Verification & Dispatch)'.
Benefits: Reduction in Idle Freight cost. Higher rake capacity utilization. Improved operational efficiency and higher turnaround. Reduced number of rake trips annually for the same tonnage. Lower carbon footprint per ton-km.
Impact: The system optimizes loading accuracy, generating ₹3.64 Cr in yearly cost savings by eliminating idle freight and maximizing rake utilization.

projects.title: Design, Development & Manufacturing of Dewatering Drum
projects.description:
Introduction: Dewatering rotating Drum is a key component in a slag granulation system that separates granulated blast furnace slag from water. It efficiently dewaters the slag, converting it into a valuable, dry product for the industry, while enabling 100% water recycling and reducing environmental impact.
Previous Practice: Proprietary item Procured from OEMs.
Proposed Solution: In-house Design, Development & Manufacturing of Dewatering rotating Drum (In TGS Manufacturing facility).
Challenges: 1. Design- OEMs design & Detail drawings not available. 2. Major material Grade and Specification not available.
Block Diagram - INBA: The diagram illustrates the INBA (Integrated Slag Granulation and Dewatering) process. 'Metal and slag tapped from tap hole (BF Bottom)' flows to 'Slagrunner'. 'Drain pit' collects water. 'Blow box' pours slag on water jet. 'Granulation tank' separates water from slurry. 'Dewatering drum' dewaters granulated slag. 'Slag Dry pit tank' collects slag. 'Recovery tank' collects overflow water. 'Emergency tank' supplies water. 'Make up from GCP' and 'Make up Water Flow' are also shown. Key components include 'Emg valve VP-7180.2', 'CP-41 A8418', 'WRP', 'PRY-0182', 'PB-0182', 'SCARI belt', 'SGOP', 'SCA#2 belt', 'Industrial water tank', 'Hot water tank', and '143'.
Schematic Diagram of INBA-II: This diagram details the INBA-II system. It includes 'GRANULATION TANK', 'COARSE FILTER', 'BLOW BOX', 'DRAIN VALVE VG7114, VG7115', 'RECOVERY TANK', 'SLAG DRY PIT TANK', 'MAKEUP WATER FROM PIEZOMETRIC TOWER', 'CP42A, B Flow=300m³/H', 'PRY1, 2 Flow=200m³/H', 'INDUSTRIAL NETWORK', 'DAMPER', 'EMERGENCY TANK', 'DEWATERING DRUM', 'HOT WATER TANK', 'PGR1, 2, 3 Flow=1000m³/H', and 'BV 7154 PB01, 02 Flow=50m³/H'.
Benefits: 1. Capability Enhancement. 2. Reduced dependency on OEMs for timely delivery. 3. Approx. 30% Cost Reduction (Rs. 1.25 Cr.). 4. Spares availability.
Way Forward: 1. Analysis of Manufacturing Stages and Learning from 1st Dewatering Drum Manufacturing. 2. Cycle Time Optimization. 3. Cost Optimization – Manufacturing. 4. Cost Optimization - Sourcing.
Impact: The in-house de-watering drum design achieves ₹1.26 Cr savings with a 30% cost reduction by eliminating OEM dependency. It ensures timely availability of critical equipment, enhances manufacturing capability and improves slag-handling reliability.

projects.title: Optimization of Zn Consumption in Ductile Iron Pipe Plant
projects.description:
Introduction: High zinc consumption in Ductile Iron pipe finishing was driven by over-application, inconsistent deposition, uncontrolled trolley movement, variable gun angles, lack of real-time monitoring, dedusting losses, and occasional gun jamming. These issues raised conversion cost/pipe, reduced yield, and strained sustainability goals. A structured, data-driven program was launched to control application length, stabilize deposition, and standardize operations across finishing lines, ensuring corrosion protection without excess material use.
Proposed System: Real-time encoder-based zinc usage tracking and visibility. Controlled forward-reverse trolley motion tuned by pipe diameter size for each finishing line. Standardize gun angle at anti-rotation of pipe and 160 mm pipe-to-gun distance for uniform 130 g/m² deposition for each pipe diameter size. Reduce excess overlap length by 200 mm/pipe. Optimize dedusting with the help of motorised valve to achieve the perfect spraying and dedusting flows and zinc-wire procurement for quality and cost.
Prototype: Small scale setup visualizing entire cycle of the Zinc coating station. Every cycle time has been scaled down chronographically from lifter up, cone clamping, lifter down and zinc gun movement. For visualisation of spray, LED lights have been used.
Benefits: Lower zinc wastage across all finishing lines due to fixed gun to pipe distance. Reduced conversion cost/pipe. (300 grams savings per pipe converts to 90 rupees per DN500 pipe savings). Lower dust wastage in area reducing environmental concerns.
Business Potential: Recurring zinc cost savings. (Annual potential of 12 cr). Lower environmental footprint. (70 MT dust reduction annually). Stronger product consistency & customer trust. (Live monitoring of zinc wire feed rate using encoder technology).
H-1 Savings Summary (in Rs Cr.): The bar chart shows savings across different periods (P1, P4, P6, H-1). Plan values are 2.00, 1.41, 0.90, 0.54, 0.16, 0.02. The H-1 savings are 3.06 and 2.33.
Way Forward: Continue size/line-wise tuning of trolley & gun settings as per the pipe diameter in the lines. Live dashboards for quick corrections. Real time monitoring of actual deposition rate (required standard- 130gm/m2).
Challenges: Hot- work in zinc rich areas. Achieving uniform deposition (130gm/m2) across all pipe diameter sizes. Paint/dust-to-paint trials failed (due to high conversion costs- 2300 rupees/ pipe higher). Ensuring power availability and pipe dia size availability during trial.
Impact: The solution cuts zinc wastage, saving ₹90 per pipe and enabling ₹1.2 Cr annual recurring savings.

projects.title: Karakuri Safety System for Fall Protection
projects.description:
Background: In the packing line at Tata Bluescope, the down ender tilts coils to a vertical position, exposing a dip pit that creates a high-risk man-fall hazard during operation. As operators work near the pit for strapping, an inherently safe, automatic pit protection system is required.
Implemented Solution: The proposed solution is a purely mechanical, zero-energy Karakuri safety gate, interlocked with the down ender through a rope-pulley mechanism. The gate automatically closes before pit exposure as the down ender tilts and reopens when the coils return to vertical. The system is inherently fail-safe, with the gate defaulting to the closed position.
Block Diagram: The block diagram illustrates the sequence: 'Down ender at rest' -> 'Downender start tilting 90° to take in the horizonal coil' -> 'Mechanical motion transfer via Rope-Pulley' -> 'Automatic safety Gate Closure' -> 'Pit Fully Isolated (Fail-Safe Condition)'.
Earlier Condition: An image shows a 'Danger Risk of falling' into a '15 Feet Deep' pit, with a coil being handled.
Post project implementation: An image shows the pit covered by an 'Automatic Safety Gate', eliminating the fall risk into the '15 Feet Deep' pit.
Challenges: Cost Constraint: Designed with minimal budget using in-house resources. In-House Development: Concept, design, and execution driven entirely by internal innovation.
Benefits: Eliminates man-machine interaction at a high-risk pit zone. Zero energy usage with no operating cost. High reliability through simple mechanical design. Low maintenance and long service life.
Business Potential: This solution can be standardized and replicated across down enders and coil-handling pits, delivering a scalable safety upgrade. It significantly reduces accident risk without adding energy consumption, automation complexity, or operating costs—making it a high-impact, low-cost safety intervention.
Impact: Enhances operator safety by eliminating exposure to open pit zones.

projects.title: Automated Sick Wagon Detection at RLS, Noamundi
projects.description:
Background: Logistics Noamundi equipped with three Rapid Loading System (RLS) having a capability of 24 MTPA, dispatches Iron ore to different customers viz., TSJ, TSK, TSG. Two types of rakes BOBSN & BOXN are used here for loading. Rakes are back pushed from exchange yard to RLS. After back pushing, wagon checking and fitting activities are being carried out before loading for identification of defective/sick wagons and unwanted/foreign materials.
Earlier Method: Wagon checking for detection of sick wagons & identification of unwanted materials were being done manually before loading. After identification of sick wagon, the person used to climb over the rake & fix a red flag, which demarcates that the wagon is sick & no loading must be done in it. The list of such wagons are then communicated to Railways for further verification. However, this process, poses a continuous risk of slip, trip & fall hazards. Also, continuous loco movement poses a fatal potential hazard for the working crew.
Implemented Method: An AI camera-based system was installed at RLS rake entry gate, which automatically detects the defective wagon and the foreign materials inside the wagons. After detection, it generates a report of wagon numbers with pictures, which is further checked by RLS control room & sent to Railways for verification. The wagon inspection report generated by AI system is validated during wagon checking & the sick wagon numbers are entered in PLC. As the sick wagon enters the loading zone, pop up with hooter is generated in PLC for sick wagon & alerts the control room operator.
Benefits: No manpower required for identification of sick wagon leading to reduction of slip, trip & fall hazards. System optimization resulted in increased efficiency in rake checking and fitting activities.
Wagon Inspection Report: A sample report shows 'Train Number: 1750251019565', 'Total Wagons: 58', 'Status: Ok', 'Defect Wagons: 0', 'Wagon Type: OPEN_WAGON'. It lists SIR NO, OR, TYPE, WAGON NO, DEFECT, DEFECT REMARKS. An image shows 'REDUCED Broken/Damaged surface detected'.
Impact: Enhances safety by removing human exposure to hazardous wagon-checking areas.

projects.title: Semi-Automated Roll-Roughing Robot Arm
projects.description:
Introduction: In Wire Rod Mill, the incoming hot billet is passed on to the consecutive stands by the rolls of the Roughing stand. It is important for the rolls of the Roughing stand to have a firm grip on the billet as there is no other mechanism for pushing the hot billet into the consecutive stands. Therefore, the surface of the roll needs to have enough friction/roughness. This roughness is created by grinding the rolls using abrasive material. Over time this roughness is lost due to wear and tear of the roll as it processes the hot billets. This smoothens the surface and grip/bite of the roll is lost. So the process of grinding needs to be repeated periodically.
Proposed Solution: Development of a semi-automatic robotic arm for grinding the rolls of the roughing stand. The robotic arm has 3 Degrees of Freedom (DoF) in the linear axes (X, Y and Z). This linear motions will be used for moving across the roll passes, shifting the grinding tool from top roll to bottom roll and either moving the tool towards the roll or retracting it. The robotic arm also has a servo arm that has 3 DoF in the rotational axes and then it has a swinging mechanism at the end of the servo arm to hold the grinding tool and apply pressure on the roll using adjustable springs. There are force sensors to accurately measure the force applied while grinding and high precision piezoelectric film sensor for measuring the grinding depth.
Block Diagram: The process starts with 'Define objective, specifications and precision of the robotic arm'. This leads to 'Selection of motion system, control method and grinding tools' and 'Design and Simulation of robot arm in cad'. Then, 'Creation of working prototype and testing and tuning' and 'Integration of control system and sensors'. Finally, 'Implement the robotic arm in WRM'.
Components of roll: The diagram shows a roll with 'Body', 'Neck', 'Wobblers', 'Collars', 'Plain roll', and 'Grooved roll'.
Conventional Process: The roll surface is roughened manually using a handheld grinding tool. One worker grinds away at the roll surface while another rotates the roll. Manual intervention is involved during the grinding and poses a safety risk as the workers need to do their job near heavy rotating machinery.
Benefits: Elimination of man machine interference thereby removing safety risk. Reducing roll grinding time and thus downtime. Accurate tracking and adjustment of grinding tool speed, force and depth of grinding. Increasing dimensional accuracy thus ensuring consistent friction across all rolls. Increasing roll lifespan by minimizing the grinding depth to just the required amount.
Uniqueness: The robotic arm will be developed with flexible and modular hardware so that it can adapted to various mills wherever grinding is required. The tools and sensors can be made to be interchangeable, and any profile can be traced via firmware variations.
Way Forward: Refining and increasing the robustness of the design and polishing the control system. Testing and tuning the robotic arm in the working environment. Implementation of the robotic arm in Wire Rod Mill.
Challenges: Ensuring the robotic arm is compact lightweight and portable and detachable. Providing smooth and jitter free motion of the motors via the controller. Ensuring proper calibration and accuracy of the sensors.
Impact: Prevents risky man-machine interaction by automating the roll-grinding process.

projects.title: Automated Wagon Cleaning System
projects.description:
Introduction: The current wagon cleaning process is labor-intensive, unsafe, and heavily dependent on manual intervention, exposing workers to dust, fumes, slips, falls, and accidents. Injury reports include 31 First-Aid cases, 11 LTIs, and 1 fatality, highlighting significant risk. The manual cycle takes 40 minutes per wagon, causes operational delays, increases tare weight due to residual material, and leads to demurrage charges. The system also demands high manpower (10-12 workers per wagon), leading to annual labor costs exceeding ₹1.24 crore.
Proposed System: The proposed solution introduces a vacuum-based automated cleaning system to eliminate human entry into wagons and reduce man-machine interface. A high-capacity suction unit removes coal, fines, dust and residual materials efficiently, supported by a cyclone separator and baghouse for dust control. It ensures safer, faster, and more consistent cleaning with minimal manual involvement.
Top View Diagram: The diagram shows a 'BOXN WAGON' on 'TRACK RAILS'. Below the wagon is a 'ROLLER BRUSH' (120 cm long). A 'VACUUM MOTOR' (30 cm) is positioned above the wagon, and a 'HOPPER' and 'DISCHARGE BIN' are located at the end of the wagon.
Benefits & Business Potential: Automation significantly reduces injuries, cleaning time, manpower dependency, and wagon damage. Reduces the tare-weight-based demurrage, with estimated savings of ~₹6 lakh. Labour cost reduction can save over ₹1.24 crore annually. Improved throughput supports faster rake turnaround, enhancing dispatch reliability. The system's modularity allows replication across RM locations.
Challenges: Integrating the proposed system automatic camera detection requires wagon floor data which is insufficient as of now.
Uniqueness: The benefits of the project makes it the only feasible automated solution for low-RL, water-accumulated sites like Noamundi while significantly reducing injury risks and operational delays.
Future Scope: The automated cleaning system can be deployed across multiple locations using standardised gantry frames, suction systems, and scrubbers. Future enhancements include IoT-based monitoring, improved water recycling, energy-efficient pumping, fully autonomous sequential wagon cleaning, and integration with yard-level dispatch systems. Scalability supports BOXN, BOXNHL and similar wagon types.
Impact: Reduces injuries and saves ₹1.24 Cr yearly through automated wagon cleaning.

projects.title: Intelligent Routing & In-Cabin Safety Alert System
projects.description:
Introduction: Delays in communicating unsafe acts on haul roads significantly reduce operator reaction time, increasing the risk of accidents and equipment damage. A real-time, on-machine alert system is needed to eliminate these delays and enhance safety. When safety warnings are not delivered instantly, operators may continue unsafe behaviors without realizing the risk—leading to situations such as vehicle collisions, haul-truck toppling, equipment damage, or serious injuries.
Pain Area: Berm disintegration or missing berms. Boulder presence on haul roads. Tailgating: Insufficient safe-distance between heavy earth moving machinery (HEMM). Poor road visibility.
Conventional Process: Manual intervention required: Daily manual-ticket logging after alerts received at the IRMSC department. Time delay in alert transmission: ~7 hours. Increased operator response time (usually in minutes) as no real-time alert is provided.
Proposed System: Real-time hazard detection using computer vision. In-vehicle alerting via LoRa WAN connectivity. This system uses advanced computer-vision technology to continuously monitor haul-road conditions and detect potential hazards in real time. Centralized monitoring with visual alert dashboards. Reduced human error through automated detection. Smart Alert Management System for prioritization.
Alert Management: The system uses a 2-level alert management strategy. 'LEVEL A: ADVISORY (LOW RISK)' involves 'ALERT DESENSITIZATION' and 'CONSEQUENCES: INCREASED ACCIDENTS'. The recipient is 'Area Manager/ Officer', delivery is via 'Dashboard/ Email/SMS', and action is 'Observation and Analysis'. 'LEVEL B: CRITICAL (HIGH RISK)' has recipients 'Nearby Vehicles and Area Manager/Officer', delivery via 'IN-CABIN ALERT', and action is 'Immediate follow up and action'.
Uniqueness: Real-time on-board alert generation. Alert generation time: 0.25 seconds. In-cabin audio visual alarm. Operator response time reduced to seconds, even milliseconds. Intelligent routing enables ultra-fast alert relay. The "Hybrid-Autonomous” strategic bridge.
Benefits: Scalable and modular solution. Minimal cost for installation. Significant improvement in haul-road safety. Reduction in property damage and operational incidents. Early warnings reduce operator reaction time and improve decision making.
Way Forward: Pilot deployment in high-risk zones. Data-driven optimization & model refinement. Expansion to additional hazard parameters and new mine areas.
Impact: Reduces reaction delays from 7 hours to 0.25 seconds, preventing haul-road incidents.

projects.title: Intelligent Rail Geometry Monitoring
projects.description:
Introduction & Problem Statement: Skip-hoist systems and rail logistics in lime plants, blast furnaces, and excavated pits serve as essential production lifelines. Tracks operate at steep inclines (high-angle) and are subjected to heavy impact loads. Derailments occur due to a combination of wheel slippage, material spillage, rail wear, and joint defects. Current maintenance practices rely on fixed-interval replacement, leading to capital waste (premature scrapping of healthy tracks) or unplanned downtime (unexpected failures). Manual inspection in high-angle or hazardous zones creates a critical man-machine interface (MMI) risk.
Proposed Solution: A single Sensor Car is used to detect all track-related defects. Two sensor categories are used: Ultrasonic and IMU Sensors. Ultrasonic sensors measure track gauge and detect widening or rail mushrooming. IMU sensor monitors cross-level, alignment, and vibration to identify track defects.
Enhanced Block Diagram: The system starts with 'COLLECT Real-Time Data' from 'Skip Car Movement on Track' using 'IMU + Ultrasonic Sensor Data Acquisition'. This data is then sent to 'STORE' in a 'Microcontroller / Data Logging Unit'. The stored data is then used to 'DISPLAY' on a 'Real-Time Dashboard & Alerts'.
Benefits: Minimizes MMI Risk: Significantly reduces the need for manual inspection on hazardous inclines, lowering the potential for accidents. Asset Life Extension: Enables a shift from time-based to condition-based maintenance, resulting in substantial Capex savings. Production Gain: Prevents unplanned derailments ("furnace starvation") and reduces slippage-related wear.
Uniqueness: Compact retrofittable sensor module. Real-time geometry analytics dashboard. Scalable and adaptable to different track systems.
The Reality: Industrial Scalability: Scaling requires the Sensor Car to maintain consistent accuracy across extended track sections. Seamless integration with existing skip or loco operations is critical for effective large-scale deployment. Wider deployment increases the need for regular sensor calibration and robust maintenance planning. Large-scale implementation produces substantial data volumes, necessitating efficient processing and reliable defect-reporting systems.
Way Forward: Pilot industrial validation at the plant. Integrate with the plant-wide monitoring system. Deploy and standardize for full-scale adoption.
Versatility & Application: Hazardous Zones: Designed for deep excavation pits and underground mines where human access is legally restricted. Remote Safety: Wireless data transmission enables operators to assess track health from a safe control room, minimizing the need for "Red Zone" entry.
Impact: Enhances system reliability by identifying track faults before derailments occur.

projects.title: Dynamic Remaining Useful Life Estimation for Motor
projects.description:
Introduction: Tata Steel lacks a dynamic, sensor-driven system to estimate the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of motors. As a result, both unexpected motor failures and premature maintenance occur, leading to significant downtime, increased maintenance cost, and suboptimal asset utilization. There is a critical need for a real-time RUL prediction framework that continuously monitors motor degradation, forecasts failure proactively, and optimizes maintenance timing.
Proposed System: A sensor-integrated predictive maintenance framework that continuously monitors motor health and predicts time-to-failure. Sensors Used: Vibration, temperature, current, and RPM. Process Flow: a. Real-time sensor data acquisition. b. ML model analysis for degradation pattern detection. c. RUL estimation and live dashboard visualization. Prototype: Small-scale motor setup with live dashboard for real-time condition monitoring and predictive insights.
Block Diagram: The 'INDUSTRIAL MOTOR TEST RIG' has 'VIBRATION SENSOR', 'TEMPERATURE' sensor, 'CURRENT' sensor, and 'RPM SENSOR'. These feed into 'DATA ACQUISITION & PROCESSING' which includes 'MICROCONTROLLER (ARDUINO UNO)', 'DATA LOGGER (SD CARD MODULE)', and 'SIGNAL CONDITIONING & FILTERING'. The processed data goes to 'PROCESSED REAL-TIME DATA' which feeds into 'RUL PREDICTION & HEALTH STATUS'. This status is used for 'REAL-TIME PLOTS & GAUGES' and 'ALERTS & NOTIFICATIONS'. The 'MACHINE LEARNING RUL MODEL' uses 'MODEL TRAINING (HISTORICAL DATA)' and 'PREDICTION ALGORITHMS (e.g., REGRESSION, DL)'.
Benefits: Reduces unplanned downtime and maintenance costs. Improves equipment reliability and operational efficiency. Provides a scalable framework for industrial predictive maintenance.
Business Potential: Applicable across Tata Steel units and other industries using rotating machinery. Reduces unexpected production halts and increases asset life. Supports transition toward Industry 4.0 predictive maintenance systems.
Way Forward: Develop and test a pilot prototype in selected plant sections. Dashboard for real-time alerts. Collaborate with maintenance teams for implementation feedback and training.
Challenges: Sensor calibration and noise interference in harsh industrial settings. Limited initial dataset for ML model training. Integration with existing IT systems. Ensuring reliability and accuracy during live operations.
Impact: Improves motor reliability by predicting failures early and reducing downtime.

projects.title: Condition Monitoring of Pellet Car
projects.description:
Introduction: The slide track and pellet car are critical parts of the induration machine in a pellet plant. It allows the pellet car to move smoothly and also helps prevent hot air from leaking from the induration zone during operation.
Business Impact: Average downtime: 25–35 hours. Direct material loss due to 24 hours downtime: ₹8.1 crore.
Equipment Wise Delay FY22-FY25: A bar chart shows delays for different equipment. Ball Mill: 438.92, Bucket elevator: 169.84, Induration machine: 77.33, Mixer: 76.12, Roksh: 40.21.
Pellet Car Equipment: The diagram shows various parts of a pellet car: 'Upper sidewall' (from high alloyed heat-resistant steel), 'Lower sidewall' (holds the pellets on the pallet car), 'Grate bar holder' (locks the grate bars), 'Roller set' (track wheels and pressure rollers), 'Sealing element and slide bar' (prevents false air suction), 'Front plate' (reversible design for docking with adjacent pallet car and for sealing purposes), 'Center casting', and 'End casting' (to mount pallet car body, side walls, sealing elements, roller sets).
Pellet equipment simulation: An image shows a 3D model simulation of pellet car movement.
Pain Points in Condition Monitoring: Slide track uprooting leads to plant breakdowns. Unexpected equipment breakdowns had occurred. Inspection is done manually through visual checks (no automation). Excessive wear and tear reduces equipment life. Difficult to identify which pellet car is defective.
Root cause analysis: The diagram 'Undesirable Loading On Slide Track' shows various causes: 'Ineffective Spring Seal (Vertical Force)', 'Excessive Floating of Reversed Pallet Cars (Horizontal Force)', 'Improper Lubrication (Frictional Force)', 'Improper Leading Edge'. These lead to 'Ingress of Pellets in the Spring Seal', 'Over Compression of Spring Seal Due to Sagging', and 'Improper Placement of Pallet Car over the Slide Track by Lifting Sprocket'.
Proposed Solution: Real-time wheel-to-wheel distance monitoring using Ultrasonic sensors to detect clearance deviation and pellet car sagging.
Block Diagram: The system starts with 'Sensor Segment', which feeds into 'Model Analysis'. This leads to 'Real time Monitoring' and 'Data Insights'. The outputs are 'Misalignment status' and 'Sagging Status'.
Solution Benefits: Identification of defective pellet cars with exact location. Risk assessment of potential failure for each pellet car. Enables predictive maintenance and action prioritization. Eliminates manual visual inspection.
Impact: Enables early defect detection, avoiding breakdowns and ensuring safer operation of Pellet Car.

projects.title: Smart Inspection System for Conveyors
projects.description:
Introduction: 1. Conveyor component failures cause belt damage, downtime & production loss. 2. Manual inspection is slow & subjective; early faults often go unnoticed. 3. Wear of idlers and pulleys produces unique acoustic patterns detectable via sensors. 4. Fixed microphones enable automated health monitoring.
Problem Statement: Undetected conveyor component failures lead to unplanned stoppages & disruptions in scheduled production.
Proposed System: 1. Microphones placed along conveyors capture idler sound continuously. 2. Local Processing Units (LPUs) analyze signals using AI/ML to detect anomalies. 3. Fault insights sent wirelessly to control room dashboard for maintenance alerts. 4. Operators can review audio logs & plan preventive maintenance.
Block Diagram: The system starts with 'Belt Conveyor (Source of Sound)'. This is captured by 'Acoustic Recording (Fixed Sensors)'. The signals undergo 'Signal Processing & Feature Extraction'. Then, 'ML Classification (Random Forest Model)' is applied. The output is 'Health Status Report (Healthy / Warning / Faulty)', which leads to 'Maintenance Action Prioritization'.
Benefits: 1. Detects faults early, preventing belt & pulley damage. 2. Enables continuous, real-time predictive monitoring. 3. Reduces manual inspections, false alarms & maintenance costs. 4. Provides objective, data-driven maintenance decisions.
Business Potential: 1. Sensors need dust/weather protection & periodic cleaning. 2. Varying load affects acoustic patterns, requires model calibration. 3. Integration with existing plant systems & networks needed.
Challenges: 1. Deployable on critical conveyors across mines & material handling units. 2. Modular & scalable architecture for plant-wide implementation. 3. Aligns with Tata Steel's digital & predictive maintenance goals.
Way Forward: For large-scale deployment, the analysis framework presently in use, can be adapted to Distributed Optical Fiber Sensing (DOFS), enabling cost-effective continuous monitoring across entire conveyor lengths.
Key Results: Achieved 88% classification accuracy, 100% fault detection, 0% false alarms during testing.
Impact: Prevents earlier undetected failures with 100% fault detection accuracy.

projects.title: Portable Cooling System for IR Cameras
projects.description:
Introduction: IR cameras on cranes help detect equipment overheating and improve visibility during operations. These cameras fail when exposed to temperatures beyond their safe operating limit. The surrounding crane-bay environment is much hotter than what the cameras can handle, leading to frequent malfunctions. High heat causes unstable readings, reduced accuracy, and early camera failure. A portable cooling system is needed to keep the cameras within a safe temperature range and ensure reliable performance.
Conventional Process: IR cameras operate directly in high-temperature crane environments. No cooling system is used to protect them from heat. Cameras often exceed safe temperature limits and fail. Breakdowns disrupt monitoring and increase maintenance cost.
Proposed Solution: Add enclosure to reduce heat exposure. Use a heat sink with industrial fans for active cooling. Use Temperature Gun for temperature Detection. Design a portable unit for easy deployment on cranes.
Block Diagram: The system consists of a 'Power Supply' connected to an 'Industrial Axial Fan' and an 'Aluminum Heat sink'. These components are housed within an 'Enclosure', which contains the 'IR camera'.
Benefits: Prevents overheating and improves camera reliability. Enhances thermal monitoring accuracy and safety. Extends camera lifespan and reduces replacement cost. Portable design allows use on multiple cranes.
Uniqueness: First portable cooling system specially designed for crane-mounted IR cameras. Compact, cost-effective, and easily deployable. Combines insulation, heat sinking, and active cooling. More efficient and lighter than commercial cooling housings.
Challenges: Extremely high ambient temperatures around the crane. Maintaining airflow in a compact portable system. Preventing heat build-up inside the enclosure. Ensuring reliability during continuous crane movement.
Way Forward: Standardize installation for all crane locations. Expand deployment across all critical monitoring zones.
Impact: Boosts camera uptime and accuracy through portable, active cooling protection.

projects.title: Automation of EOT Crane With Wheel Collar Monitoring
projects.description:
Introduction: Manual EOT crane operation and inspection increase safety risks and maintenance effort. Lack of real-time wheel collar monitoring leads to unexpected failures and downtime. A remote-controlled crane with real-time monitoring enables safer, smarter, and predictive maintenance.
Conventional Process: Manual EOT crane operation requires operators near the equipment. Wheel-collar inspections are done manually during shutdowns. Wear or misalignment is detected late, causing unplanned downtime.
Proposed Solution: Remote-controlled EOT crane with cameras and sensors for safe operation and real-time monitoring from a control station. Laser- and camera-based wheel-collar monitoring for continuous wear detection without stopping crane operation.
Block Diagram of Wheel Collar: The diagram shows a wheel with dimensions D1 and D2, and an angle theta. The formula 1-(d1 + d2 cos theta) = t is provided, likely for calculating wear or clearance.
Benefits: Improved safety by reducing direct man-machine interaction during operation and inspection. Cost savings up to ₹26 lakhs through condition-based maintenance. Reduced reliance on third-party inspections with an in-house, patent-ready solution.
Innovation: Innovation is not adding new tech – it's solving existing problems smarter. This project shifts crane inspection from manual to predictive. Global leaders like Panasonic, Apple, Tesla, and Amazon innovate to stay ahead – and so does Tata Steel through projects like this. Digitalization is the starting point; innovation decides who finishes first. Safer, smarter, faster inspections through remote operation and real-time monitoring. Moves maintenance from reactive to preventive, reducing downtime and improving reliability.
Way Forward: Plant-level implementation using PLC-based control, with validation of real-time wheel collar monitoring under actual crane operating conditions.
Impact: Reduces unsafe manual crane checks and saves up to ₹26 lakh through early fault detection.

projects.title: AI-Based Machine Endurance & Diagnosis System
projects.description:
Introduction: Tata Steel lacks a dynamic, sensor-driven system to estimate the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of motors. As a result, both unexpected motor failures and premature maintenance occur, leading to significant downtime, increased maintenance cost, and suboptimal asset utilization. There is a critical need for a real-time RUL prediction framework that continuously monitors motor degradation, forecasts failure proactively, and optimizes maintenance timing.
Proposed System: A sensor-integrated predictive maintenance framework that continuously monitors motor health and predicts time-to-failure. Sensors collect machine health data: Temperature, Vibration, Voltage imbalance, Insulation resistance. PLC (Main Brain) controls machines and ensures real-time safety. AI Engine (Python-based) analyzes historical data. HMI / Dashboard displays: Machine health status, Remaining Useful Life (RUL), Fault warnings.
Block Diagram: The system starts with 'SENSOR ASSEMBLY' which feeds 'DATA ACQUISITION & PROCESSING'. This data goes to 'PLC-CORE CONTROL' and 'DISPLAY'. The 'DISPLAY' shows 'SELF-HEALING LOGIC' and 'COMPONENT IDENTIFICTIVE ACTION'. 'DATA ACQUISITION & PROCESSING' also feeds into 'AI MODEL (EDGE PROCESSING)' which uses 'LSTM', '1D-CNN MobileNetV2/XGboost' for 'AI PREDICTIONS: Bearing Failure in 48h'. This leads to 'FAULT DETECTION' and 'PREDICTION'. 'PLC-CORE CONTROL' also feeds into 'SAFETY, ALARMS, LIVE READINGS'. 'AI PREDICTIONS' and 'PLC-CORE CONTROL' feed into 'DECISION EXECUTION'. 'DECISION EXECUTION' leads to 'PLC EXECUTING CORRECTIVE ACTION' and 'HUMAN VERIFICATION'. The entire process is a 'CONTINUOUS MONITORING CYCLE'. The color legend indicates Blue for Sensing/Control, Green for Data/AI Processing, and Red for Decision/Action.
Benefits: Early fault detection. Reduced unplanned downtime. Increased machine life. Lower maintenance cost.
Challenges: Initial data availability. Sensor reliability. Legacy system integration.
Way Forward: Live plant data integration. AI self-learning models. ERP / CMMS integration.
Impact: Enhances equipment reliability by predicting faults and extending machine life.

projects.title: RF-Based Remote Track Switching System
projects.description:
Problem Statement: The plant has 165 track switching points. There are 120 daily trips using 42 torpedoes. Each rake completes an average of 2.8 trips per day. Each trip passes through 7-8 switching points, and manual switching results in approximately 17 minutes of idle time per trip. Ground crew members must dismount to change track points, creating exposure to movement-related risks. Level-crossing safety is managed manually by the ground crew. There are approximately 11 vulnerable areas with unbarricaded level crossings.
Fatality Incident: A fatality incident of a ground crew has happened at HML during manual operation at night. (Late G C Verma, P. No 114895).
Recorded Recent STF Cases: On 25-Apr-2023, IP-1 slipped while stepping down from loco, injuring his right ankle. (IN00164001). On 15-Feb-2024, IP-2 fell while climbing stairs of loco#65. (IN00184517). On 18-Aug-2024, IP-3 slipped and fell down near the track while going to switch the point#19. (IN00198337).
Proposed System: Automated Drop-gate System (Pre-requisite): Robust and reliable IP68 rated proximity sensor that will trigger alarm and drop-gate in uncrowded areas.
Smart Remote For Track Switching: Enables remote operation of track switches without requiring the locomotive to stop. The system comprises of an RF transceiver at both the remote unit and the switch point, an Arduino UNO, an OLED display on the remote, and a pushbutton interface for switch activation. Includes an LED indicator to inform the locomotive operator of the current track alignment. Uses the NRF24L01+ module with unique ID assignment for each receiver. Incorporates fool-proof addressing to prevent incorrect switching due to proximity or overlapping signals. Programming involves interfacing the RF module with an Arduino board and developing the control logic using the RF24 library within the Arduino IDE environment.
Block Diagram For Remote Track Switching: 'Operator selects the specific motor point using' a 'Push button is input to Arduino, which generates output signal to NRF24L01+ transceiver module'. 'This makes the ID of TX & RX same'. 'Signal sent from transmitter module'. 'Receiver catches that signal as input to the microcontroller'. 'Microcontroller generates signal to operate the relay connected to motor'. 'Track switched, confirmation via LED'. 'Command to shift or remain in same line'.
Fig. 1: Circuit diagram of automatic drop-gate in prototype: Shows an Arduino UNO connected to a motor, a push button, and an NRF24L01+ module.
Fig. 2: Circuit diagram of remote-based track switching: Shows a 'REMOTE' unit with an Arduino and NRF24L01+ connected to a push button and OLED. 'MOTOR POINT 1', 'MOTOR POINT 2', 'MOTOR POINT 3' units each have an Arduino and NRF24L01+ connected to a motor and LED.
Fig. 3: Working prototype of proposed solution: An image shows the physical setup of the remote track switching system.
Benefits: Enhanced ground crew safety. Reduction in halt timing by ~17 minutes per trip. Improved TLC turnaround from 2.9 to 3.2. Refractory lining life will increase. Projected increase (from increase in turnaround) in daily hot metal carrying capacity by (340*42*0.3=) 4,284 tonnes/day or 15.63 lakh tonnes annually.
Challenges: Proper sensor placement and Mounting. Subjected to Packet Loss. Protection of sensors from dust/water/heat. Needs repeated testing to make sure there's no chance of derailment.
Future Scope: Mesh-Networking of wireless nodes. Remote monitoring/SCADA integration. Predictive Maintenance Analytics.
Impact: Enhances torpedo-movement safety by eliminating risky manual track switching.

projects.title: Smart TLC Coupling Confirmation System
projects.description:
Introduction: Currently, coupler lock engagement between Torpedo Ladle Cars (TLC) and locomotives is verified manually, often in conditions with dust, fumes, or low visibility. This manual verification increases the risk of errors, potentially leading to decoupling failures and roll-down accidents.
Proposed System: Laser and LDR module senses and indicates the alignment status of the coupler. Proximity sensor confirms physical lock engagement. Automated safety interlock operated through a solenoid actuator. Powered by a battery that can self-recharge via an axle-driven dynamometer.
Block Diagram: The system flow starts with 'Coupler Alignment Sensing'. This leads to 'Lock Engagement Confirmation'. This confirmation triggers 'Signal triggers actuation of interlock'. The system is powered by 'Power through axle driven dynamometer' and uses a 'Solenoid actuator inserted'.
Benefits: Eliminates human dependency and manual errors by providing active barriers for reliable coupling. Prevents roll-down and derailment incidents, addressing a red-risk process hazard. Self-powered design with minimal infrastructure requirements.
Way Forward: Horizontal deployment across all locations. Can become a standard safety interlock for rail-based logistics.
Challenges: Maintaining sensor efficiency and accuracy under high vibration and dusty conditions.
Impact: Eliminates manual coupling errors, preventing decoupling failures and accidents.

projects.title: Vacuum Circuit Breaker Closing & Tripping Test Jig
projects.description:
Introduction: VCB is a three phase HT switch. During its maintenance, trial and testing is needed several times. Closing and tripping actions are done outside of its panel by opening live HT panel control power (110VDC). This test jig provides stand alone power supply for safe operation of the VCB/MOCB outside of its panel.
Proposed System: Proposed VCB testing system Jig is made of PVC for shock free and light weight. It is a portable power supply system. This test jig consists of 2 MCB one for main incoming supply and other for spring charging of VCB. 2 indication lights, one for incoming supply ON and other for system ready to operate. 2 push buttons for closing and tripping of VCB. By using this test jig now VCB testing can be done without opening live HT panel.
Business Potential: This idea can be horizontally deployed across all location where VCB testing is performed.
Benefits: Management always motivates us to work safely; therefore, this test jig minimizes high risk and unsafe work. Thus, making the process safe. This is a portable system that allows us to conduct VCB testing anywhere, there is no longer a need for any additional external special power source to perform these tests.
Challenges: Our primary challenge was to eliminate unsafe practices and transit to a safe process. Working on HT live panels is extremely hazardous and carries a high risk of fatal accidents; therefore, ensuring safety was our biggest priority.
Way Forward: By implementing this idea, we can either establish our own test jig manufacturing plant or sell the patent to an existing Test Jig manufacturing company.
Impact: Enables safe VCB testing without opening live HT panels, reducing accident risks.

projects.title: Active Strip Sway Correction at Entry Looper
projects.description:
Introduction & Problem Statement: The Entry Looper of PLTCM, CRM TSJ ensures continuous strip flow between the entry section and pickling section by maintaining a buffer that compensates for speed differences. The tension imbalance at the entry looper lead to strip shifting and swaying. Current practice involves slowing down of line on the basis of the output given by Laser Distance Meter (LDM) sensors installed on the looper car.
Loss Incurred: Time (FY25): 12.1 hours. Production loss equivalent to 3.12 KT. Opportunity cost amounting to 1.4 crores.
Proposed Solution: We propose a modification in the looper car frame design by extending the looper car into a 'Hydraulic Car' that accommodates a hydraulic power pack to actuate Roll #3. The formula for theta (Yaw angle) is given as: theta = (2/3) * (LF / (4ML^2 + 0.75MD^2))^(1/3), where V is speed of the sheet over the roll (mm/min), L is length of the roll, F is force of actuation, M is mass of the roll, D is diameter of the roll, and X is deviation from center in mm.
Solution Concept Diagram: The diagram shows a 'Hydraulic cylinder' connected to a 'PP (PowerPack)'. This system is integrated with a 'Hydraulic car' and 'Looper car', with rolls #1, #2, #3 indicated.
Block Diagram: 'Strip Deviation detection via LDM sensors' feeds into a 'Digital Controller'. The controller sends signals to a 'Servo Valve', which controls a 'Hydraulic Cylinder'. The 'Hydraulic Cylinder' provides feedback via 'LVDT' to the 'Digital Controller', and also performs 'Actuation'.
Strip Shifting of wider coils: An image shows 'Coll shift Detection System on looper car at PLTCM' with measurements of 400 mm, 9 mm, and 415 mm, indicating 'Drive Side' and 'Work Side'. This is the 'LDM sensor output'.
Benefits/ Business Potential: Improves strip stability, enabling higher looper utilization (>50%) for wider coils (width > 1400 mm). Minimizes strip damage, resulting in significant cost savings across the supply chain.
Way Forward & Challenges: Implement the proposed active strip sway-correction system at CRM-TSJ on the entry looper and monitor its impact on strip stability and looper utilization. Implementation of this solution will require a temporary plant shutdown and a significant capital investment.
Impact: Improves looper utilization and recovers ₹1.4 Cr lost productivity annually.

projects.title: Sensor based stability system for steel transfer car
projects.description:
Introduction: Steel Plant faces steel/slag car derailments caused by slag jamming on the track or wheel imbalance. This year in LD#1, steel car derailments occurred twice, each causing a delay of nearly 24 hours. Each vessel taps approximately 30 heats in 24 hours, resulting in an opportunity loss of 30 heats per derailment.
Proposed System: Install smart tilt sensors on the steel car bogies. An embedded controller continuously monitors sensor inputs using threshold-based anomaly detection. The system sends an alert or triggers an auto-stop command before a derailment can occur.
Block Diagram: The system starts with 'Installation of sensors on car'. This feeds into 'Derailment risk detection algorithm'. The algorithm triggers 'Alert/Auto Stop System'. Finally, 'Monitoring (LD Control Room)' is performed.
Prototype: An image shows the 'ESP32 TILT SAFETY CONTROL' dashboard with 'ROLL: 0.2', 'PITCH: -0.1', 'TRIP: SYSTEM NORMAL', and 'DIRECTION: STOP'. Buttons for 'SETTINGS', 'STOP', 'FORWARD', 'RESET' are also visible. This is the 'Tilt Sensor Dashboard'.
Benefits & Business Potential: Prevents ₹2.40 crore loss per derailment (₹4.80 crore in two incidents). Reduces 24-hour production downtime from each derailment. Prevents molten metal accidents & improves worker safety. Auto stop before derailment.
Challenges: High temperature and dust area.
Future Scope: Potential to integrate with IoT and AI systems for predicting and detecting slag accumulation on the track, advancing the system beyond emergency stopping. Scalability: The solution can be rapidly deployed across all LD shops and other steel plant units.
Impact: Eliminates derailment events, saving ₹2.4 Cr each event and improving safety.

projects.title: Hybrid Field Assisted Fuel Conditioner for Rolling Mills
projects.description:
Introduction: All thermal utilities generate heat via combustion. Combustion efficiency depends on fuel atomization and air-fuel mixing.
Proposed System: Magnets break large clusters into smaller groups. FIR ceramics chamber heat/excite that mist, so it ignites instantly. Copper/Electrostatic forces cause those groups to repel each other into a fine mist.
Block Diagram: The system starts with 'Fuel source', which goes through a 'Filter'. From the filter, it goes to a 'Homogenizing chamber', then a 'Pre-heating chamber', then a 'Magnetic resonator', and finally an 'FIR Ceramic chamber'. The 'Pre-heating chamber' also feeds into a 'Re-Heating Furnace', and the 'FIR Ceramic chamber' feeds into an 'Electrostatic Copper Grid'.
Benefits: Improves combustion efficiency, resulting in approximately 4–8% fuel savings. Helps reduce air pollution, supports pollution-control efforts, and contributes to climate-change mitigation. Quick to install and requires no major modifications to existing equipment.
Carbon footprint stats: For every 1 Liter per Metric Ton (L/Mt) improvement in fuel efficiency, you save approximately: 3.1 kg of CO2 for every ton of product produced.
Business potential: Fuel Savings: 3-5%. Quick Installation & Payback. No Major System Modifications.
Challenges: Performance varies by fuel type. Requires precise sizing & placement. Needs baseline fuel data for validation.
Way Forward: Can be used in any plant where combustion processes are involved.
Impact: Reduces fuel use by 4-8% resulting lower CO2 emissions & enhances sustainable operations.

projects.title: Digital Engineering Initiatives in Engineering&Projects
projects.description:
Introduction: Tata Steel recognized to modernize its Engineering and Projects (E&P) processes to overcome traditional challenges and enhance operational excellence. The company embarked on a journey to implement Digital Engineering initiatives. This strategic shift was driven by the need to mitigate inherent risks, improve efficiency, and foster greater accuracy across complex industrial projects—ultimately transforming conventional engineering workflows into a more integrated, digital, and collaborative environment.
Proposed System: 3D Model-Based Engineering: Starts with 3D block layouts and develops into detailed structural, mechanical, piping, and electrical models, reaching ~90% VR-ready integration. Design Automation: Automates 2D drawing annotations and detailing directly from the 3D model, reducing manual effort and errors. Virtual Construction (4D/5D): Provides schedule validation, constructability checks, and dynamic clash detection through time-lapse simulations. Smart P&ID: Digital P&ID data enables one-click extraction, smooth 3D integration, and automatic document generation. Discrete Event Simulation: Analyzes resource usage, identifies bottlenecks, and optimizes throughput for logistics and material handling.
Benefits: Design Accuracy: Reduces errors, ensures robust designs. Project Efficiency: Faster schedules, increased productivity, reduced costs. Operational Excellence: Confident decisions, risk mitigation, improved throughput. Data Integration: Accurate P&ID, 3D model links, validated designs. Cost Efficiency: Significant man-day savings through automation. Unified Collaboration: Autodesk Docs for seamless data, standardized workflows.
Business Potential: Reusability of 3D models: Will ensure significantly faster design cycles for repetitive projects. Diverse Use Cases: 3D Modeling, Virtual Construction, DES, and Smart P&ID applied extensively. In-house Innovation: Custom solutions like Design Automation reduce external dependency.
Way Forward: Integration with AI: Evolve current 3D workflows toward AI-driven design automation and analysis. Proprietary Development: Continued investment in in-house software (Design Automation). Proactive Clash Management: Online system for tracking and resolving engineering clashes.
Challenges: Design & Model Discrepancies: Errors, missing parts, misalignments, drawing mismatches identified. Effort-driven Job: 3D & 4D activities require higher manpower & high effort for review and validation. Continuous Refinement: Ongoing need for continuous validation and process improvements.
Impact: Enhances sustainability by reducing design rework, material waste, and project delays through accurate 3D modelling and automation.
